title: Week 7: Presentation of PCIbex (Your first questionnaire)
text:
This week, we will introduce the web-based platform PCIbex, which is used to design online questionnaires and experiments. By the end of this week, you will have the script of your very first experiment ready!

🎯 Goals & layout🧠 Key concepts💻 Necessary codes📝 Homework


What to expect from the platform:

Layout: What does this platform look like? Where do you type the script?
Assistance: How to access the documentation and the user forum.
Overall Presentation: Getting comfortable with the interface.
Basics: Setting up your first experiment from scratch.



Writing a simple yet powerful script:

Comments: How to comment a script on PCIbex to keep track of your structure.
Elements: How to create texts, tasks, and visual components.
Adjustments: How to adjust these elements to make them behave exactly as you want.
Logging: How to ensure the relevant results are stored correctly on PCIbex.



A brief summary of the essential elements:

newTrial(...): Used to introduce a trial in the experiment.
newText(...): Used to introduce a line of text.
newTextInput(...): Used to prompt the participants to type their response.
newButton(...): Used to insert a button in the experiment.
newDropDown(...): Used to prompt the participants to select between several options.
newScale(...): Used to display a scale on the screen.
SendResults(...): Used to send the responses to the PCIbex server.



  Download the full script 


  Experiment from the script covered this week 



Please complete the following tasks before our next meeting:

Download the (simple) script of this week
Annotate each line of code (use your own words, these annotations are for your own reference!)
Upload the annotated script on Moodle (you can upload the .js file, or a Word or PDF file)

title: Week 7: Presentation of PCIbex (Your first questionnaire)
text:
1. Presentation of the Interface
Before we begin coding, you need to access the platform and set up your workspace.

  Go to PCIbex (farm.pcibex.net) 


⚠️ Important: Please don’t forget to verify your email after signing up, otherwise you will only be allowed to create one experiment!

Explore the different parts of the PCIbex interface below:

1. Getting Started2. The Project Banner3. Folders & Files4. Coding Interface5. Actions Menu


The first thing to do is to create an account (click on “Sign up”). Once you have created your personal account, you can log in.





After logging in, you will see your dashboard. On the left side, you can find your projects. On the right side, you can find templates for specific paradigms.
Today, we will learn how to code a simple experiment from scratch. Let’s click on “Empty project”.







Once inside, you will see the main workspace.





Can you see where it is written Empty project_copy? Click on it to change its name. Good to know: The participants won’t see this name when you share the experiment, so you can be very explicit with your naming conventions!







This section contains two main parts:

Resources: So far, this is empty. This is where you upload files for your experiment (images, audio, or a .csv file where all your items can be found—we will talk about that next week!).
Scripts: There is already a file called main.js. For simple experiments, this is enough. This is the file we modify to code the whole experiment.








Once you open a script or an Excel-like file, you can modify it directly here.
The best feature is that you can test your experiment anytime after modifying it just by clicking “Refresh”. (Be patient, it can take several seconds!)







The last part is the “Actions” menu, which gives you several options:

“Unpublished” button: Once your experiment is ready, click this before starting data collection! You can test your script as many times as you wish beforehand, and those test data will be stored separately.
Results: Where you can find the downloaded data of your experiment.
Share: Click this to obtain the public link to share your experiment online.

title: Week 7: Presentation of PCIbex (Your first questionnaire)
section: 2. Let’s be organized: The structure of your experiment
text:
2. Let’s be organized: The structure of your experiment

2.1 Conceptual structure
Before you start typing any code, you must have a crystal-clear vision of the procedure for your experiment. Even though every experiment is unique, most follow a standard logical sequence:





flowchart LR
  A([👋 Welcome Page]) ==> B([🛠️ Practice Trials])
  B ==> C([🧪 Experimental Trials])
  C ==> D([🏁 Goodbye Page])
  
  style A fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#3498db,stroke-width:2px
  style B fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#2ecc71,stroke-width:2px
  style C fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  style D fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#95a5a6,stroke-width:2px






The difference between experiments lies in the specific content of each of these blocks. You must be very explicit about what each section consists of before you begin.






✍️ Preparatory Activity: Map it out!



A highly effective way to visualize your overall procedure is to draw it by hand.
Sketch out a series of rectangles on a piece of paper, with each rectangle corresponding to a different step (Consent, Background, Instructions, etc.). Once you have drawn the whole experiment, your only remaining task is to “translate” those boxes into PCIbex code!





2.2 Create the structure on PCIbex: Comment lines
Now that we have our structure, let’s put it into PCIbex. Before writing the actual experiment commands, it is best practice to create a “skeleton” of your script using comments.

Open the main.js file in the PCIbex coding interface.
Delete all the default text except the very first line (PennController.ResetPrefix(null)).
Build your outline using comments. In JavaScript, anything following two slashes // is ignored by the computer.

Here is what you need to add. (Notice the green lines indicating what you should type):
  PennController.ResetPrefix(null)

+ /// 1. Welcome page
+ // 1.1 Hello page, ICF
+ 
+ // 1.2 Background questionnaire
+ 
+ // 1.3 Instructions
+ 
+ /// 2. Practice trials
+ 
+ /// 3. Experimental trials
+ 
+ /// 4. Goodbye page






⚠️ Important Note!



In the examples on this website, the + sign simply highlights that a line is a new addition to the script. You should not actually type the + sign!


Once you finish, your clean, organized main.js script should look exactly like this:
PennController.ResetPrefix(null)

/// 1. Welcome page
// 1.1 Hello page, ICF

// 1.2 Background questionnaire

// 1.3 Instructions

/// 2. Practice trials

/// 3. Experimental trials

/// 4. Goodbye page

title: Week 7: Presentation of PCIbex (Your first questionnaire)
text:
3. Creation of your first experiment
Now we will build the actual content of the experiment step-by-step. Before that, we will need some basics:

We will be taking about “functions”, which are also refered to as elements in PCIbex. An element comes with a name and parentheses, such as: newText(). The details to input in the parentheses.
PCIbex is a platform to run experiments, and therefore it requires trials. Therefore, we always need to start with the newTrial() element, even if it is not a trial in the proper sense.


📄 3.1 Welcome page

A. Welcome pageB. Background informationC. Instructions


Texts and buttons
The welcome page consists of paragraphs with: - Welcoming words, - The Informed Consent Form, - Buttons to continue to the next pages, - Another page to collect personal information.
Let’s start with the welcome page, and as mentioned above, with a newTrial() element. We want to give a name to this element, and then add text, as follows.
/// 1. Welcome page
// 1.1 Hello page, ICF

newTrial("Welcome page",
+ newText("This is the welcome page.")
+ ,
+ newText("You can type the Informed Consent Form here.")
)
When you run these lines, you will realize that it does not work at all. And this is normal, it is just because we did not specify what to do with all these elements! We need to add commands to these elements. What we want is that these texts are (a) displayed (i.e., printed) on the screen, and that it does not automatically run into the next step. Here is the updated version:
/// 1. Welcome page
// 1.1 Hello page, ICF

newTrial("Welcome page",
    newText("This is the welcome page.")
+     .print()
+     .center() //You can add more commands like that
    ,
    newText("You can type the Informed Consent Form here.")
+     .print()
+     .center()
+     .wait() //Please remark that this command is placed at the last element
)
We are running into a new problem. The procedure “waits” as we asked to, but how to go to the next page? We can solve this by adding a “Continue” button (using the newButton() element).
/// 1. Welcome page
// 1.1 Hello page, ICF

newTrial("Welcome page",
    newText("This is the welcome page.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("You can type the Informed Consent Form here.")
        .print()
        .center()
-        .wait()
+   ,
+    newButton("Continue")
+        .center()
+        .print()
+        .wait() //Please remark that we moved the 'wait' command here
)
And we are done with this first page!


Texts, text inputs and dropdown
Next, we turn to the page where we collect personal information, such as the age of the participants, the highest degree they obtained, the languages they speak, etc. We already know how to type some text, so let’s start with this:
// 1.2 Background questionnaire

newTrial("Background information",
    newText("We will ask you some questions about your background. Please feel free to skip some questions if you do not wish to answer.")
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait() 
    )
Now we need to add some text to prompt the participants to enter their age, and a box where they can directly write. We will proceed this way:
// 1.2 Background questionnaire

newTrial("Background information",
    newText("We will ask you some questions about your background. Please feel free to skip some questions if you do not wish to answer.")
        .print()
        .center()
-        .wait()
+    ,
+    newText("Your age:")
+        .print()
+        .center()
+    ,
+    newTextInput("Age") //Please not that we added "Age" just to refer back to this element later
+        .print()
+        .center()
+        .wait()
    )
Let’s say that we want to collect more data, but we want the participants to choose between different options. We will do that with the newDropDown() element, as follows:
// 1.2 Background questionnaire

newTrial("Background information",
    newText("We will ask you some questions about your background. Please feel free to skip some questions if you do not wish to answer.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Your age:")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newTextInput("Age")
        .print()
        .center()
-        .wait()
+    ,
+    newText("Your highest degree:")
+        .print()
+        .center()
+    ,
+    newDropDown("Degree", "Please select one option")
+        .add("Elementary school", "Junior high school", "Senior high school", "BA", "MA", "Ph.D.")
+        .print()
+        .center()
+        .wait()
    )
You can add as much as you want now that you understand the logic.
⚠️ Important! In the end, we will need to add a “Continue” button. If not, it will move to the next screen after the participants selected one option. Adding this button prevents such potential problems.
// 1.2 Background questionnaire

newTrial("Background information",
    newText("We will ask you some questions about your background. Please feel free to skip some questions if you do not wish to answer.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Your age:")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newTextInput("Age")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Your highest degree:")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newDropDown("Degree", "Please select one option")
        .add("Elementary school", "Junior high school", "Senior high school", "BA", "MA", "Ph.D.")
        .print()
        .center()
-        .wait()
+    ,
+    newButton("Continue")
+        .center()
+        .print()
+        .wait()
    )
The story is not over yet, since we need to specify what information needs to be stored. We cover this point in Section 4.


Nothing new!
Now that you know how to add text, you just need to follow the same logic to add the instructions! Here is an example for this course:
// 1.3 Instructions

newTrial("Instructions",
    newText("This is the instruction page.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("In this experiment, you will ... [Please enter the instructions depending on your experiment].")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Example: In this experiment, you will read sentences on the screen. These sentences will appear one at a time. Your task is to judge these sentences [add a word about how to judge the sentences].")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Here is an example: 'I will must do it.' This sentence is not acceptable, please click on 'not acceptable'.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Please click on 'I understood' if you read and understood the instructions.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newDropDown("InstructionsCheck", "Please select one option")
        .add("I read and understood the instructions.", "I did not read the instructions.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newButton("Continue")
        .center()
        .print()
        .wait()
    )



We just finished the first part of the experiment! The good news is that this is actually the most tedious coding part, so it will only get easier for the rest of the coding!


🧪 3.2 The experiment itself
Since many students decided to use written sentences as their experimental materials, the coding of the script for this experiment will be introduced based on this.

A. Practice trialsB. The experiment itself


Texts and scales
Let’s start with something simple: How to display an experimental sentence. We already know how to do it, like below.
/// 2. Practice trials
newTrial("Practice",
    newText("FirstPractice", "This is a grammatical sentence")
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
    )
There is something different from before. We assigned a name to the newText() element (here, FirstPractice) such that we can keep track of the results from each sentence.
Let’s assume that this is an acceptability judgment experiment, using a 7-point Likert scale. Here is how to add this scale.
/// 2. Practice trials
newTrial("Practice",
    newText("FirstPractice", "This is a grammatical sentence")
        .print()
        .center()
-        .wait()
+    ,
+    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
+        .labelsPosition("bottom")
+        .print()
+        .center()
+        .wait()
    )
We just need to use the newScale() element, give it a name, and enter the scale itself. The command .labelsPosition() is used to display the parts of the scale on the screen. Finally, we can add what the labels mean to remind the participants how they are supposed to judge the sentences.
/// 2. Practice trials
newTrial("Practice",
    newText("FirstPractice", "This is a grammatical sentence")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
+        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
+        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
    )
We are almost done. We need to add two screens: One before the practice trials to inform the participants that the practice trials are about to begin, and another one after, to mention that the practice is over and that the participants need to get ready before the official experiment begins.
/// 2. Practice trials

+ newTrial("AnnouncementPractice",
+     newText("The practice trials are about to begin.")
+         .print()
+         .center()
+     ,
+     newButton("Continue")
+         .center()
+         .print()
+         .wait()
+ )

newTrial("Practice",
    newText("FirstPractice", "This is a grammatical sentence")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
    )
    
+ newTrial("AnnouncementEndPractice",
+     newText("The practice is over. Please click on 'Continue' to start the experiment.")
+         .print()
+         .center()
+     ,
+     newButton("Continue")
+         .center()
+         .print()
+         .wait()
+ )


Same as the practice trials, but there are more items!
Official trials need to exhibit the same format, so we need to use the exact same code, and change the variable names in addition to the experimental materials.
/// 3. Experimental trials

newTrial("Experiment",
    newText("FirstSentence", "This is another grammatical sentence")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
    )
    
newTrial("Experiment",
    newText("SecondSentence", "This sentence not is grammatical")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
    )
This is quite straightforward. But imagine if you have 140 sentences, then you will need to repeat this 140 times. In addition, presenting them in a random order using this method is not easy. We will see next week how to use a trick from PCIbex to make this simple.





👋 3.3 The goodbye page
The “goodbye page” is just a straightforward newText() element.
/// 4. Goodbye page

newTrial("Goodbye",
    newText("GoodbyeMessage", "Thank you very much for your participation!")
        .print()
        .center()
        )
There is a simple yet important trick we can play with. We can actually control when the results are sent to the server. Let’s say we only want the data of the participants who completed the experiment (and not of people who gave up in between). There is an element called SendResults(). We just need to place it right after the last trial and before the goodbye page to do so!
/// 4. Goodbye page

+ SendResults()

newTrial("Goodbye",
    newText("GoodbyeMessage", "Thank you very much for your participation!")
        .print()
        .center()
        )

title: Week 7: Presentation of PCIbex (Your first questionnaire)
text:
4. Adjusting the script
Once the basic structure is working, we need to fine-tune what data we are collecting and how the experiment looks to the participants.

4.1 What results to store? The log function
Let’s run the script we wrote so far. By the end of the experiment, there is a screen saying that it successfully sent the information to the server. We just need to click on Results and download the file. Here is what we obtain.




Click on the image to zoom in


We can indeed find all the elements we wrote… and yet, we cannot see the answers for the personal information, or the acceptability data!






No panic. This is only because we did not ask PCIbex to store such data, only to display them.
We will have to use the .log() command to ensure PCIbex records the exact data points you need for the analyses.



Here is the final script updated with the .log() commands.






Click to unpack the whole code





PennController.ResetPrefix(null)

/// 1. Welcome page
// 1.1 Hello page, ICF

newTrial("Welcome page",
    newText("This is the welcome page.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("You can type the Informed Consent Form here.")
        .print()
        .center()
,
    newButton("Continue")
        .center()
        .print()
        .wait()
)

// 1.2 Background questionnaire

newTrial("Background information",
    newText("We will ask you some questions about your background. Please feel free to skip some questions if you do not wish to answer.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Your age:")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newTextInput("Age")
        .print()
        .center()
+        .log("final")
    ,
    newText("Your highest degree:")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newDropDown("Degree", "Please select one option")
        .add("Elementary school", "Junior high school", "Senior high school", "BA", "MA", "Ph.D.")
        .print()
        .center()
+        .log()
    ,
    newButton("Continue")
        .center()
        .print()
        .wait()
    )

// 1.3 Instructions

newTrial("Instructions",
    newText("This is the instruction page.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("In this experiment, you will ... [Please enter the instructions depending on your experiment].")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Example: In this experiment, you will read sentences on the screen. These sentences will appear one at a time. Your task is to judge these sentences [add a word about how to judge the sentences].")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Here is an example: 'I will must do it.' This sentence is not acceptable, please click on 'not acceptable'.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Please click on 'I understood' if you read and understood the instructions.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newDropDown("InstructionsCheck", "Please select one option")
        .add("I read and understood the instructions.", "I did not read the instructions.")
        .print()
        .center()
+        .log()
    ,
    newButton("Continue")
        .center()
        .print()
        .wait()
    )

/// 2. Practice trials

newTrial("AnnouncementPractice",
    newText("The practice trials are about to begin.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newButton("Continue")
        .center()
        .print()
        .wait()
)

newTrial("Practice",
    newText("FirstPractice", "This is a grammatical sentence")
        .print()
        .center()
+        .log()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
+        .log()
    )
    
newTrial("AnnouncementEndPractice",
    newText("The practice is over. Please click on 'Continue' to start the experiment.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newButton("Continue")
        .center()
        .print()
        .wait()
)

/// 3. Experimental trials

newTrial("Experiment",
    newText("FirstSentence", "This is another grammatical sentence")
        .print()
        .center()
+        .log()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
+        .log()
    )
    
newTrial("Experiment",
    newText("SecondSentence", "This sentence not is grammatical")
        .print()
        .center()
+        .log()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
+        .log()
    )


/// 4. Goodbye page
SendResults()
newTrial("Goodbye",
    newText("GoodbyeMessage", "Thank you very much for your participation!")
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
        )



Now we can see the results! (As you can see, it is not an effective way to store the actual sentences that have been displayed. Again, we will cover a much more efficient way to solve this problem next week)




Click on the image to zoom in




4.2 Details of the procedure and the aesthetics
We covered the basics of experiment building on PCIbex. So far, we have a very functional script, but it is not very appealing. There are many options to change the aesthetics of the experiment, and I invite you to have a look at the PCIbex documentation.
Alternatively, I found that AI tools can be quite useful to improve the overall aesthetics. You can always have a try, and now that you understand the basics of the script, you know what it changed, and how to change back or adjust if it is not your taste!






Caution



Always be careful when using AI to change the aesthetics, as it is not completely naive. Changing the size of the text, the fonts, the color, or anything, may have an impact on the results. In addition, AI may change some codes such that in the end the results are not properly saved. Always check, double check, triple check!



  Example of an AI-augmented aesthetics 







Click here to see the AI-augmented script – Same experiment, different aesthetics

title: Week 2: What is language, and ethics in linguistic research
text:
During Week 2, we will talk about ethics in linguistic research, covering the following topics:

1. Outstanding questions2. Ethical Practices3. Institutions


What is language, and where is language?

What are the consequences of these answers when designing a linguistic survey?
How do our assumptions about “where language lives” change how we ask questions?



What are ethical (or respectful) practices?

We will define what it means to be ethical in linguistic surveys (and any other kind of survey).
I will share real examples of ethical dilemmas and situations that I encountered in the past few years, and how to avoid such problems.



What are the institutional structures for research ethics in Taiwan?

We will focus on the Center for Taiwan Academic Research Ethics Education (in Chinese: 臺灣學術倫理教育資源中心) provided by the Ministry of Education.
I will show you why you should care about these regulations, even as undergraduate and graduate students.










  Center for Taiwan Academic Research Ethics Education 

(臺灣學術倫理教育資源中心)










📝 Assignment for Next Week



For students enrolled in the program (and if you have time):
Please take the mandatory online ethics classes and pass the examination. (6 hours of training)

Submission: You do not need to upload the certificate of results.
Verification: I will provide a sheet on Moodle; you will just need to check the box next to your name to confirm you have finished.

title: Week 8 - PCIbex: Multiple-item questionnaires, Latin square and other paradigms
text:
This week, we will discuss how to set the parameters of a template to obtain a more simple and yet more powerful script. By the end of this week, you will have the script of your very final experiment ready!

🎯 Goals & key concepts💻 Necessary codes📝 Homework


How to optimize your script:

Template: How to create a script that can run automatically through all the trials?
Randomization: How to present the trials using a random order?
Counterbalancing: How to counterbalance the items using a Latin square design?



A brief summary of the essential elements:

Template(...): Used to introduce a trial template.
Sequence(...): Used to control the order of the different parts of the experiment.
randomize(...): Used to present the trials using a random order.
newVar(...): Used to create a new variable.
getX(...): Used to obtained the content given by the participants in a specific element (example: getTextInput(...), getDropDown(...), etc.)
getVar(...): Used to obtain the content of an element stored in a variable (i.e., within newVar(...)).



  Download the full script 


  Experiment from the script covered this week

Download the script of this week
Annotate each line of code (use your own words, these annotations are for your own reference!)
Upload the annotated script on Moodle (you can upload the .js file, or a Word or PDF file)

title: Week 8 - PCIbex: Multiple-item questionnaires, Latin square and other paradigms
section: 1. Let’s simplify and automatize the process
text:
1. Let’s simplify and automatize the process

1.1 Creation of the template
So far, we created an experiment following this structure:





flowchart LR
  A([👋 Welcome page]) ==> B([🛠️ Practice trials])
  B ==> C([🧪 Experimental trials])
  C ==> D([🏁 Goodbye page])
  
  style A fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#3498db,stroke-width:2px
  style B fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#2ecc71,stroke-width:2px
  style C fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  style D fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#95a5a6,stroke-width:2px






But more precisely, it looks like that, with multiple trials:





flowchart LR
  A([👋 Welcome page]) ==> B([🛠️ Practice trials])
  
  subgraph Exp [🧪 Experimental trials]
    C1([Trial 1]) --> C2([Trial 2]) --> C3([Trial 3]) --> C4([Trial 4]) -.-> C5([Last Trial])
  end
  
  B ==> C1
  C5 ==> D([🏁 Goodbye page])
  
  style A fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#3498db,stroke-width:2px
  style B fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#2ecc71,stroke-width:2px
  style D fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#95a5a6,stroke-width:2px
  
  style C1 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  style C2 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  style C3 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  style C4 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  style C5 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  
  style Exp fill:none,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5






As we saw last week, we could type all the trials individually. Alternatively, there is a much more efficient way to go. We provide the details below.

A. Create the material tableB. Update the existing code


Instead of typing the trials in the script, we create a table where we can list them. Let’s say we have 8 sentences, divided between 2 conditions (so 4 sentences per condition)




ItemNo
Sentence
Condition




1
This is the first sentence
Grammatical


2
This is the second sentence
Grammatical


3
This is the third sentence
Grammatical


4
This is the fourth sentence
Grammatical


5
This are the fifth sentence
Ungrammatical


6
This are the sixth sentence
Ungrammatical


7
This are the seventh sentence
Ungrammatical


8
This are the eighth sentence
Ungrammatical



We need to have this tables under the Resources part on the left of the interface. One way to do is to open an Excel document, save it into a .csv file (columns seperated by commas), and upload on PCIbex. This is the most convenient way to go, especially when you have a lot of information.
Instead, we are going to create it directly for this tutorial. Right next to the Resources part, you can click on the + (Create new file) sign, and you rename it to: material.csv. You will see the logo changing to something similar to: .
After that, you click on the file, and it will open it next to the script. You just have to copy and paste the following section inside (the commas , indicate different columns).
ItemNo,Sentence,Condition
1,This is the first sentence,Grammatical
2,This is the second sentence,Grammatical
3,This is the third sentence,Grammatical
4,This is the fourth sentence,Grammatical
5,This are the fifth sentence,Ungrammatical
6,This are the sixth sentence,Ungrammatical
7,This are the seventh sentence,Ungrammatical
8,This are the eighth sentence,Ungrammatical


Now that we have our materials uploaded onto PCIbex, we just need to modify the script that we already have. As a reminder, this is what it looked like with two trials:






Previous script





/// 3. Experimental trials

newTrial("Experiment",
    newText("FirstSentence", "This is another grammatical sentence")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
    )
    
newTrial("Experiment",
    newText("SecondSentence", "This sentence not is grammatical")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
    )



We actually just need to update that a little bit… and you will realize that it will even look easier than what we wrote! More specifically:

We must specify that we are dealing with a template using the Template() function. The content of the template (i.e., the trials) is found within the parentheses.
We must also specify the name of the file where the trials can be found, we change the file into a variable to refer to (here, the name of the variable is row), and which column corresponds to the trials to display on the screen (here, the column is called Sentence; row.Sentence means that it is the column called Sentence in the file corresponding to the variable row).
Importantly, we delete all other newTrial() elements. We only need one within the template.

/// 3. Experimental trials

+ Template("material.csv", row => 
newTrial("Experiment",
-    newText("FirstSentence", "This is another grammatical sentence")
+    newText("Sentence", row.Sentence)
        .print()
        .center()
        .log()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
        .log()
    )
+ ) // This is the closing parenthesis corresponding to `Template()`

- newTrial("Experiment",
-     newText("SecondSentence", "This sentence not is grammatical")
-         .print()
-         .center()
-     ,
-     newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
-         .labelsPosition("bottom")
-         .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
-         .after(newText("Acceptable"))
-         .print()
-         .center()
-         .wait()
-    )
And that’s all, we are done!






1.2 log() with a template
If you run the experiment and look at the results file, you will see that you can find the judgments given by the participants. But we may want more information in the results file:

The ID of the sentence,
The sentence itself,
The experimental condition,
And other information found in the material table.

/// 3. Experimental trials

Template("material.csv", row => 
newTrial("Experiment",
    newText("Sentence", row.Sentence)
        .print()
        .center()
-        .log() // Since we know it does not work, let's delete it
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
        .log()
    )
+    .log("ItemNo", row.ItemNo)
+    .log("Sentence", row.Sentence)
+    .log("Condition", row.Condition)
)
In addition, since the participants provide background information, we would like to add these details in the same line as well! But such information is not found in a seperate file, they are provided by the participants themselves. This is therefore a two-step process to store such information in the results file, as follows:







flowchart LR
  A(["👋 Welcome page <br> <b>Define the variable</b>"]) ==> B(["🧪 Experimental trials <br> <b>Log the data</b>"])
  
  style A fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#3498db,stroke-width:2px
  style B fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#2ecc71,stroke-width:2px









Step 1 - Define the variable when the participants provide their information. We create a new variable (think as if we create a new column) called “Age” with the newVar() element. Then we set the content of this variable. We need to tell PCIbex where to find it with the getTextInput() element. In the same code, we have an element called newTextInput("Age"), therefore we need to use the same name: getTextInput("Age"). We need to do the same with other elements we want to keep, hence the getDropDown("Degree") element.

// 1.2 Background questionnaire

newTrial("Background information",
    newText("We will ask you some questions about your background. Please feel free to skip some questions if you do not wish to answer.")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newText("Your age:")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newTextInput("Age")
        .print()
        .center()
        .log("final")
    ,
    newText("Your highest degree:")
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newDropDown("Degree", "Please select one option")
        .add("Elementary school", "Junior high school", "Senior high school", "BA", "MA", "Ph.D.")
        .print()
        .center()
        .log()
    ,
    newButton("Continue")
        .center()
        .print()
        .wait()
+    ,
+    newVar("Age")
+        .global()
+        .set(getTextInput("Age"))
+    ,
+    newVar("Degree")
+        .global()
+        .set(getDropDown("Degree"))
    )

Step 2 - Store the information along with the results of each trial. We just need to add two lines, starting with the .log() command that we are very familiar with. We need to use the getVar() element to say “grasp the information from this variable”.

/// 3. Experimental trials

Template("material.csv", row => 
newTrial("Experiment",
    newText("Sentence", row.Sentence)
        .print()
        .center()
        .log()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
        .log()
    )
    .log("ItemNo", row.ItemNo)
    .log("Condition", row.Condition)
+    .log("Age", getVar("Age"))
+    .log("Degree", getVar("Degree"))
)

title: Week 8 - PCIbex: Multiple-item questionnaires, Latin square and other paradigms
section: 2. Let’s specify the template and the overall procedure
text:
2. Let’s specify the template and the overall procedure
We are almost done with the parameters of the experiment! Now that we are using the Template() element, we can set additional parameters.

A. Sequence and randomizationB. Counterbalancing


We can specify the sequence of the whole experiment right at the beginning of the script. In other words, we want to translate this figure into PCIbex language.





flowchart LR
  A([👋 Welcome page]) ==> B([🛠️ Practice trials])
  
  subgraph Exp [🧪 Experimental trials]
    C1([Trial 1]) --> C2([Trial 2]) --> C3([Trial 3]) --> C4([Trial 4]) -.-> C5([Last Trial])
  end
  
  B ==> C1
  C5 ==> D([🏁 Goodbye page])
  
  style A fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#3498db,stroke-width:2px
  style B fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#2ecc71,stroke-width:2px
  style D fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#95a5a6,stroke-width:2px
  
  style C1 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  style C2 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  style C3 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  style C4 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  style C5 fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px
  
  style Exp fill:none,stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5






We can do that using the Sequence() element, as below:
Sequence("Welcome page", "Background information", "Instructions", "AnnouncementPractice", "Practice", "AnnouncementEndPractice", "Experiment", "Goodbye")
But we will run into a problem. We did not specify when to send the results! To do that, we must give a name to the SendResults() element that we placed right before the ‘goodbye page’, and include it in the Sequence() element.
PennController.ResetPrefix(null)

+ Sequence("Welcome page", "Background information", "Instructions", "AnnouncementPractice", "Practice", "AnnouncementEndPractice", "Experiment", "Send" "Goodbye")

/// 1. Welcome page
// 1.1 Hello page, ICF

newTrial("Welcome page",
...
    )

// 1.2 Background questionnaire

newTrial("Background information",
...
    )

// 1.3 Instructions

newTrial("Instructions",
...
    )

/// 2. Practice trials

newTrial("AnnouncementPractice",
...
    )

newTrial("Practice",
...
    )
    
newTrial("AnnouncementEndPractice",
...
)

/// 3. Experimental trials
Template("material.csv", row => 
newTrial("Experiment",
...
)

/// 4. Goodbye page
+ SendResults("Send")

newTrial("Goodbye",
...
        )
With the Sequence() element, we just need to specify which part of the experiment needs to be presented in random order, using the randomize() element, as below.
Sequence("Welcome page", "Background information", "Instructions", "AnnouncementPractice", "Practice", "AnnouncementEndPractice",
+ randomize("Experiment"), 
"Send", "Goodbye")


Our last step concerns Latin square designs, i.e., when we want to counterbalance the items and present different ones to different participants for some reasons. So far, we have 8 sentences dispatched into 2 conditions, so 4 items per condition. Now, let’s say that we only want to present the two first sentences from each condition to Participant 1, and the last two sentences to Participant 2, and so on.
With PCIbex, we can specify the lists directly in the material table. We just need to have a column called group, and PCIbex will automatically identify that these corresponds to different lists in a Latin square design. My habit is to use ‘A’, ‘B’, etc. to specify the lists, such that there is no confusion with the item IDs.

In the end, the content of material.csv looks like this:
ItemNo,Sentence,Condition,group
1,This is the first sentence,Grammatical,A
2,This is the second sentence,Grammatical,A
3,This is the third sentence,Grammatical,B
4,This is the fourth sentence,Grammatical,B
5,This are the fifth sentence,Ungrammatical,A
6,This are the sixth sentence,Ungrammatical,A
7,This are the seventh sentence,Ungrammatical,B
8,This are the eighth sentence,Ungrammatical,B
Finally, to keep track of which list the participants saw, we need to use the .log() command!
/// 3. Experimental trials
Template("material.csv", row => 
newTrial("Experiment",
    newText("Sentence", row.Sentence)
        .print()
        .center()
    ,
    newScale("Scale", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7")
        .labelsPosition("bottom")
        .before(newText("Not acceptable"))
        .after(newText("Acceptable"))
        .print()
        .center()
        .wait()
        .log()
    )
    .log("ItemNo", row.ItemNo)
    .log("Condition", row.Condition)
    .log("Sentence", row.Sentence)
+    .log("List", row.group)
    .log("Age", getVar("Age"))
    .log("Degree", getVar("Degree"))
)

section: 3. Final test: Run the experiment several times and look at the results file
text:
3. Final test: Run the experiment several times and look at the results file
A good practice, when everything is set, is to run the experiments several times as if we were pretending that we are different participants. We can first check that this is indeed what we want the participants to see, and also that the results file is in order.
I just ran the experiment two times, here is what the results file look like. It is also a good practice to prepare a checklist, such that we know what to expect.






Checklist for the results file




Are there two participants?
4 trials per participant?
Trials from different lists/group?
Can I see the sentence? The ItemNo? The condition?
Can I see the age of the participants? Their degree?
Can I see their choice/judgment?
Were the sentences randomly presented?
Other checks we can think of.






Click on the image to zoom in


Note that I rearranged the results file to fit in one image. It looks quite disorganized, but we will learn in the last part of this course how to handle that without headaches.

title: Introduction to Language Questionnaires
text:
📋 Course Description
This course introduces the fundamentals of online language questionnaires with an emphasis on hands-on practice. After discussing ethic issues in linguistic research, the course will be divided into three parts:

Fundamental concepts and practices in language questionnaires.
Set-up of a web-based questionnaire (using PCIbex).
Handling, analysis, and visualization of the results (using R).

By the end of the course, students are expected to acquire the basic knowledge of questionnaire design concepts and the capacity to design and run their own language questionnaire.

title: Week 3: Fundamentals of questionnaire design
text:
This week, we will cover the basics of questionnaire (or experimental) design. We will learn the technical terms to describe the methodology employed in a questionnaire or experiment, which will be essential for you when reading academic papers, attending conferences, and writing your own final term papers.

🧪 Core concepts📚 Required readings


Experimental design variables & structure
We will discuss the following core concepts through several studies involving language:

Variables: Independent variable(s) and dependent variable(s).
Types of design: Within-participants design, between-participants design, and mixed designs.
Factorial design: What “factors” and “levels” actually mean.
Counterbalancing: Why there is a need to counterbalance, and how Latin square designs work to achieve it.



Discussion material for this week
Our discussion of these concepts will be based on the following two readings:

Abbuhl et al. (2014) (from the book “Research Methods in Linguistics”)
Rogers & Révész (2020) (from the book “The Routledge Handbook of Research Methods in Applied Linguistics”)







Here is a visual breakdown to help you synthesize the experimental design concepts we are discussing this week:

















Concept
Definition
Example application




Independent variable
The variable that is manipulated by the researcher, also known as the variable of influence. It is expected to bring about variation or change in the dependent variable.
Corrective feedback


Dependent variable
The variable that is measured or influenced.
Grammatical development


Between-participants design
A design where each level of the independent variable is comprised of different participants.
Typically developing (TD) children and children with specific language impairment (SLI)


Within-participants design
A design where the same individuals are assigned to all levels of the independent variable.
A class studying half of target items under a spaced-short condition and the other half under a spaced-long condition


Mixed design
A design that combines both within-participants and between-participants factors.
Examining L2 performance using task complexity (within-participants) and modality (between-participants)


Factorial design
A research design that includes more than one independent variable to examine separate and combined effects.
Investigating the effects of wh-dependency and dependency length on sentence processing using a 2 x 2 design


Counterbalancing
Having different, randomly assigned participants complete the different levels of the independent variable in different orders to address order or carryover effects. A Latin-square design is frequently used for this purpose to control against test- and task-order effects.
Counterbalancing four different tasks across four groups in a Latin-square design so each group completes tasks in a different order










📝 Assignment for next week



Please complete the following tasks before our next meeting:

Brainstorm: Review the concepts we’ve been through today. Start thinking of your own research question and how to translate it using the variables and design structures we’ve learned this week.
Read: Prepare the materials for Week 4:

Schütze & Sprouse (2014)
Kaiser (2014)

title: Week 4: Paradigms and tools
text:
This week, we will talk about the different types of paradigms and tools that we can use when running online questionnaires or experiments. Last week, our discussion focused on the Independent Variable (IV) and how its manipulation creates different kinds of design. This week, we shift our focus to the Dependent Variable (DV).

🧪 Core concepts📚 Required readings


Focusing on the Dependent Variable (DV)
We will explore the following key areas:

Operationalization: What makes a DV “good” (i.e., testable and measurable)?
Offline methods: Judgment experiments, ad-hoc reaction times, and various tasks for language experiments.
On-line methods: Exploring timing methods and tracking techniques.
Paradigms: We will examine common experimental setups used to elicit specific data.



Discussion material for this week
Our discussion of these concepts will be based on the following two readings:

Schütze & Sprouse (2014) (from the book “Research Methods in Linguistics”)
Kaiser (2014) (from the book “Research Methods in Linguistics”)











⚠️ Important clarification: “On-line”



Pay attention! In experimental linguistics and psychology, the word “on-line” does not mean “on the internet.” Instead, it means measuring a cognitive process “in real time” as it unfolds.













Concept
Definition / Focus
Example application




Operationalization (Dependent Measures)
The selection of a specific behavioral response to measure cognitive systems, such as time, error rate, or reported perceptions of acceptability.
Measuring reaction times as an indication of processing complexity


Off-line methods
Methods that provide crucial information about final interpretations, or the final outcome of language processing.
Acceptability judgment tasks, where speakers are explicitly asked to report their spontaneous reaction concerning whether a string of words is a possible utterance


On-line methods
Methods that tap into real-time aspects of language processing. These methods detect rapid, transient effects that are often not explicitly noticed by language users.
Using an eye-tracker to record eye movements that are closely time-locked to the speech stream as a participant listens to instructions


Reaction-time paradigms
Methods measuring how rapidly people perform different kinds of linguistic tasks.
A self-paced reading task where participants press a button to reveal words one by one, allowing researchers to record how much time is spent on each part of the sentence


Visual-world paradigm
An eye-tracking method where a participant is presented with auditory linguistic stimuli coupled with objects or a visual display.
Measuring fixation probabilities to target objects (like a beaker) and competitor objects on a computer screen as the participant hears the spoken word unfold










📝 Assignment for next week



Please complete the following tasks before next week:

Read: Review the assigned materials:

Gillioz & Zufferey (2021)

Project Planning: Seriously start to think of your final project. Consider your specific topic and decide whether you will be working individually or in a group.
